retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthesia Techniques in Dentistry and Oral Surgery -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: A known allergy to a local anesthetic agent or an ingredient of the anesthetic solution contraindicates its use. Allergy is the only absolute contraindication to local anesthesia. However, some anesthetic agents or techniques should be avoided or used with caution in certain individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Overview of Periodontal Surgical Procedures -- Complications -- Pocket Reduction Surgeries
retrieved_docs.content: Teeth with exposed root surfaces may develop dentinal hypersensitivity, causing sharp pain in response to cold, hot, or highly osmotic foods. While minor tooth sensitivity is common after most dental procedures, sensitivity after periodontal surgery can be pronounced and may require intervention. Treatment options for dentinal hypersensitivity include over-the-counter and in-office desensitizing agents, the application of sealants, or, in severe cases, root canal therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Actinic Cheilitis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Non-surgical treatments for AC include topical therapies such as 5-fluorouracil, imiquimod, trichloroacetic acid, ingenol mebutate, diclofenac, phototherapy, topical DNA enzyme repair creams, photoprotection, and curettage with methyl aminolevulinate cream/daylight PDT. [3] Many of the topical treatments cause adverse effects such as inflammation, crusting, and pain, which may reduce patient compliance.

retrieved_docs.title: Teeth Polishing -- Technique or Treatment -- Advantages
retrieved_docs.content: The main advantage of air-powder polishers is their ability to efficiently remove biofilm without harming the periodontal soft tissues or hard tissue structures. This method is faster and provides more patient comfort. It saves time, and hence, it can prevent patient and operator fatigue. It can be used on orthodontically bracketed teeth as it does not physically damage or disturb the bands, wires, or bonding cement. It reduces dentinal sensitivity by blocking the tubular opening with bicarbonate crystals. It reaches inaccessible areas that cannot be reached by rotary devices. [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Laser Carbon Dioxide Resurfacing -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: For the treatment of premalignant lesions and actinic damage, CO2 lasers may not be as effective as other methods, such as chemical peels or dermabrasion. When compared with topical 5-fluorouracil and trichloroacetic acid chemical peels, CO2 laser treatments have been shown to be equally effective in reducing the number of premalignant actinic keratoses and keratinocyte skin cancers that ultimately develop. [9] However, trichloroacetic acid has the lowest cancer incidence and the highest patient satisfaction rates compared with other modalities. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Arsenical Keratosis(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment of arsenical keratosis is not mandatory; instead, the goal is to provide supportive and symptomatic care to alleviate the patient's discomfort. There are no universally accepted standard guidelines for the treatment of arsenical keratosis. Treatment should be tailored to the patient's needs, disease severity, comorbidities, and pain tolerance. Various treatment options that have been explored over the years include: [1] [6] Cryotherapy Curettage and electrodessication Carbon dioxide laser ablation Topical keratolytic agents, such as salicylic acid (5% to 10%) and urea (10% to 20%) Imiquimod 5% cream Topical 5-fluorouracil Oral retinoids Surgical excision

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1676
retrieved_docs.content: Mandelic acid adverse reaction refers to a harmful or unwanted response that occurs as a direct result of exposure to mandelic acid, indicating that mandelic acid itself is the causative agent of this reaction. Recognizing mandelic acid as the trigger is essential for clinicians when evaluating patients who experience skin irritation, allergic responses, or other negative effects after using products containing this alpha‑hydroxy acid. Proper identification of mandelic acid as the underlying cause guides appropriate management, such as discontinuing the offending agent and providing symptomatic treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1246
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids, weak (group I), Corticosteroids, potent (group III), and Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV) are all classified as plain corticosteroid dermatological preparations. These three potency groups represent increasing levels of anti‑inflammatory strength, allowing clinicians to select the appropriate topical corticosteroid formulation based on the severity of the skin condition and the desired therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1965
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroid dermatological preparations, plain encompass a range of topical corticosteroid strengths that are classified by potency groups. Within this category, “Corticosteroids, weak (group I)” represent the low‑potency end of the spectrum, while “Corticosteroids, very potent (group IV)” represent the high‑potency end. This hierarchical classification allows clinicians to select an appropriate plain corticosteroid preparation based on the severity of the skin condition, aiming to achieve therapeutic effectiveness while minimizing the risk of adverse effects associated with higher‑potency steroids.
